[METHODS_SUMMARY] Each network node of a blockchain may use a true random number generator (TRNG) and hash algorism to fulfil the PoR protocol. In this study, we give the consensus mechanism of the PoR protocol, and classify it into a new kind of randomized algorithms called Macau.

Besides, we give some advantages of integrating quantum random number generator (QRNG) chips into hardware wallets, and also discuss the way to cooperate with quantum key distribution (QKD) technology.
[CONCLUSION_SUMMARY] In this study, we give the consensus mechanism of the PoR protocol, and classify it into a new kind of randomized algorithms called Macau. target_text: This paper proposes a blockchain consensus method called proof of randomness. Instead of making participants compete through heavy computation or coin ownership, each node uses true random numbers and hashing to help decide who can add to the chain. The authors argue that this could make block creation fairer and much less energy intensive than proof-of-work systems. They also frame the method as part of a new class of randomized algorithms, which they call Macau algorithms, and discuss possible links to quantum random-number hardware and quantum key distribution.

[INTRO_SUMMARY] Structural Hole (SH) theory states that the node which acts as a connecting link among otherwise disconnected communities gets positional advantages in the network. Motivated from the success of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on various graph mining problems, we design a Graph Neural Network-based model, GNN-SHS, to discover SHSs in dynamic networks, aiming to reduce the computational cost while achieving high accuracy.
[METHODS_SUMMARY] These nodes are called Structural Hole Spanners (SHS). Motivated from the success of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on various graph mining problems, we design a Graph Neural Network-based model, GNN-SHS, to discover SHSs in dynamic networks, aiming to reduce the computational cost while achieving high accuracy.
[RESULTS_SUMMARY] Motivated from the success of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on various graph mining problems, we design a Graph Neural Network-based model, GNN-SHS, to discover SHSs in dynamic networks, aiming to reduce the computational cost while achieving high accuracy. We analyze the efficiency of the proposed model through exhaustive experiments, and our results show that the proposed GNN-SHS model is at least 31.8 times faster and, on an average 671.6 times faster than the comparative method, providing a considerable efficiency advantage.
[CONCLUSION_SUMMARY] SHSs have many applications, including viral marketing, information dissemination, community detection, etc. Discovering SHSs is an NP-hard problem, due to which, instead of discovering exact k SHSs, we adopt a greedy approach to discover top-k SHSs.
target_text: This paper focuses on finding structural hole spanners, the nodes that act as bridges between otherwise weakly connected groups in a changing network. Because finding the exact best set is computationally hard, the authors train a graph neural network to identify strong candidates across sequences of network snapshots. The method is designed for dynamic networks rather than fixed ones, which makes it closer to real social and information networks. In experiments, the proposed model was dramatically faster than the comparison method while still aiming to recover the most important bridge nodes.

[TITLE] Acceleration of Gossip Algorithms through the Euler-Poisson-Darboux Equation
[ABSTRACT] Gossip algorithms and their accelerated versions have been studied exclusively in discrete time on graphs. [INTRO_SUMMARY] As machine learning models become more general, we need to characterise them in richer, more meaningful ways. To do so, the authors introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance.
[METHODS_SUMMARY] We describe a method to infer the cognitive profile of a system from diverse experimental data. To do so, the authors introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance.
[RESULTS_SUMMARY] To do so, the authors introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance. We showcase the potential for capability-oriented evaluation.
[CONCLUSION_SUMMARY] Using the Bayesian probabilistic programming library PyMC, we infer different cognitive profiles for agents in two scenarios: 68 actual contestants in the AnimalAI Olympics and 30 synthetic agents for O-PIAAGETS, an object permanence battery. We showcase the potential for capability-oriented evaluation.
target_text: This paper proposes a Bayesian way to infer what capabilities a system likely has from how it performs across many tasks. Instead of relying on a single overall score, the method models how task features interact with hidden abilities and then estimates a richer cognitive profile. The authors demonstrate the approach on AnimalAI competitors and on agents tested with object-permanence tasks, showing that the resulting profiles are more informative and more predictive than simple benchmark averages. The main contribution is an evaluation framework for describing systems in terms of capabilities rather than just total performance.

[INTRO_SUMMARY] Virtual-to-physical address translation is a critical performance bottleneck in paging-based virtual memory systems. The Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) accelerates address translation by caching frequently accessed mappings, but TLB misses lead to costly page walks.
[METHODS_SUMMARY] Hardware approaches enhance TLB reach through system-level support, while software optimizations include TLB prefetching, replacement policies, superpages, and page size adjustments. Prefetching Page Table Entries (PTEs) for future accesses reduces bottlenecks but may incur overhead from incorrect predictions.
[RESULTS_SUMMARY] Integrating an Agile TLB Prefetcher (ATP) with SBFP optimizes performance by leveraging page table locality and dynamically identifying essential free PTEs during page walks. Predictive replacement policies further improve TLB performance.
[CONCLUSION_SUMMARY] Predictive replacement policies further improve TLB performance. CHiRP, tailored for L2 TLBs, surpasses other policies by leveraging control flow history to detect dead blocks, utilizing L2 TLB entries for learning instead of sampling.

The paper also includes simulations showing that these continuous models closely track the behavior of the original discrete algorithms.

[INTRO_SUMMARY] As machine learning models become more general, we need to characterise them in richer, more meaningful ways. To do so, the authors introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance.
[METHODS_SUMMARY] We describe a method to infer the cognitive profile of a system from diverse experimental data. To do so, the authors introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance.
[RESULTS_SUMMARY] To do so, the authors introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance. We showcase the potential for capability-oriented evaluation.
[CONCLUSION_SUMMARY] Using the Bayesian probabilistic programming library PyMC, we infer different cognitive profiles for agents in two scenarios: 68 actual contestants in the AnimalAI Olympics and 30 synthetic agents for O-PIAAGETS, an object permanence battery. We showcase the potential for capability-oriented evaluation.
target_text: This paper proposes a Bayesian way to infer what capabilities a system likely has from how it performs across many tasks. Instead of relying on a single overall score, the method models how task features interact with hidden abilities and then estimates a richer cognitive profile. The authors demonstrate the approach on AnimalAI competitors and on agents tested with object-permanence tasks, showing that the resulting profiles are more informative and more predictive than simple benchmark averages. The main contribution is an evaluation framework for describing systems in terms of capabilities rather than just total performance.

Instead of directly pruning weights, ARMOR factorizes each weight matrix into a 2:4 sparse core wrapped by two low-overhead, block diagonal matrices. These wrappers act as efficient pre and post-transformation error correctors, offering greater flexibility to preserve model quality compared to conventional 2:4 pruning techniques. target_text: This paper introduces FAVE, a recommendation method designed to generate the next item in a user sequence more efficiently than diffusion-style approaches. The authors argue that many flow-based recommenders still waste computation because they start from uninformative noise and spend effort modeling transitions that are partly deterministic. FAVE instead learns a direct one-step trajectory from a more informative starting point, using a two-stage training process that first builds a stable semantic space and then adds a semantic anchor prior plus a learned average velocity. The goal is to keep strong recommendation quality while reducing generation cost. In simple terms, the paper tries to make sequence recommendation both faster and more purposeful by starting closer to where the answer should be.
prediction: This study is about FAVE: Flow-based Average Velocity Establishment for Sequential Recommendation. Generative recommendation has emerged as a transformative paradigm for capturing the dynamic evolution of user intents in sequential recommendation. To address these limitations, the authors present a Flow-by AverageVelocity Establishment (Fave) framework for one-step generation recommendation that learns a direct trajectory from an informative prior to the target distribution. target_text: This paper studies the Subset Balancing problem and gives new algorithms based on lattice reductions. The key result is a reduction from subset balancing with coefficients in a bounded integer range to a single instance of an infinity-norm shortest vector problem. Using the best known lattice algorithms for that target problem, the authors obtain running times that depend on the dimension but no longer pay the usual log d cost in the exponent. For sufficiently large coefficient ranges, they also show the problem becomes polynomial-time solvable. The paper is mainly a complexity-theory advance, but the practical message is that a careful geometric reformulation can lead to substantially better algorithms than the classic meet-in-the-middle approach for many parameter settings.

[METHODS_SUMMARY] Each network node of a blockchain may use a true random number generator (TRNG) and hash algorism to fulfil the PoR protocol. In this study, we give the consensus mechanism of the PoR protocol, and classify it into a new kind of randomized algorithms called Macau.

[CONCLUSION_SUMMARY] In this study, we give the consensus mechanism of the PoR protocol, and classify it into a new kind of randomized algorithms called Macau. target_text: This paper proposes a blockchain consensus method called proof of randomness. They also frame the method as part of a new class of randomized algorithms, which they call Macau algorithms, and discuss possible links to quantum random-number hardware and quantum key distribution.

[INTRO_SUMMARY] Structural Hole (SH) theory states that the node which acts as a connecting link among otherwise disconnected communities gets positional advantages in the network. Motivated from the success of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on various graph mining problems, we design a Graph Neural Network-based model, GNN-SHS, to discover SHSs in dynamic networks, aiming to reduce the computational cost while achieving high accuracy.
[METHODS_SUMMARY] These nodes are called Structural Hole Spanners (SHS). Motivated from the success of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on various graph mining problems, we design a Graph Neural Network-based model, GNN-SHS, to discover SHSs in dynamic networks, aiming to reduce the computational cost while achieving high accuracy.
[RESULTS_SUMMARY] Motivated from the success of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) on various graph mining problems, we design a Graph Neural Network-based model, GNN-SHS, to discover SHSs in dynamic networks, aiming to reduce the computational cost while achieving high accuracy. We analyze the efficiency of the proposed model through exhaustive experiments, and our results show that the proposed GNN-SHS model is at least 31.8 times faster and, on an average 671.6 times faster than the comparative method, providing a considerable efficiency advantage.
[CONCLUSION_SUMMARY] SHSs have many applications, including viral marketing, information dissemination, community detection, etc. Discovering SHSs is an NP-hard problem, due to which, instead of discovering exact k SHSs, we adopt a greedy approach to discover top-k SHSs.
target_text: This paper focuses on finding structural hole spanners, the nodes that act as bridges between otherwise weakly connected groups in a changing network. Because finding the exact best set is computationally hard, the authors train a graph neural network to identify strong candidates across sequences of network snapshots. The method is designed for dynamic networks rather than fixed ones, which makes it closer to real social and information networks. In experiments, the proposed model was dramatically faster than the comparison method while still aiming to recover the most important bridge nodes.

[INTRO_SUMMARY] As machine learning models become more general, we need to characterise them in richer, more meaningful ways. To do so, the authors introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance.
[METHODS_SUMMARY] We describe a method to infer the cognitive profile of a system from diverse experimental data. To do so, the authors introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance.
[RESULTS_SUMMARY] To do so, the authors introduce measurement layouts that model how task-instance features interact with system capabilities to affect performance. We showcase the potential for capability-oriented evaluation.
[CONCLUSION_SUMMARY] Using the Bayesian probabilistic programming library PyMC, we infer different cognitive profiles for agents in two scenarios: 68 actual contestants in the AnimalAI Olympics and 30 synthetic agents for O-PIAAGETS, an object permanence battery. We showcase the potential for capability-oriented evaluation.
target_text: This paper proposes a Bayesian way to infer what capabilities a system likely has from how it performs across many tasks. Instead of relying on a single overall score, the method models how task features interact with hidden abilities and then estimates a richer cognitive profile. The authors demonstrate the approach on AnimalAI competitors and on agents tested with object-permanence tasks, showing that the resulting profiles are more informative and more predictive than simple benchmark averages. The main contribution is an evaluation framework for describing systems in terms of capabilities rather than just total performance.

[INTRO_SUMMARY] Virtual-to-physical address translation is a critical performance bottleneck in paging-based virtual memory systems. The Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) accelerates address translation by caching frequently accessed mappings, but TLB misses lead to costly page walks.
[METHODS_SUMMARY] Hardware approaches enhance TLB reach through system-level support, while software optimizations include TLB prefetching, replacement policies, superpages, and page size adjustments. Prefetching Page Table Entries (PTEs) for future accesses reduces bottlenecks but may incur overhead from incorrect predictions.
[RESULTS_SUMMARY] Integrating an Agile TLB Prefetcher (ATP) with SBFP optimizes performance by leveraging page table locality and dynamically identifying essential free PTEs during page walks. Predictive replacement policies further improve TLB performance.
[CONCLUSION_SUMMARY] Predictive replacement policies further improve TLB performance. CHiRP, tailored for L2 TLBs, surpasses other policies by leveraging control flow history to detect dead blocks, utilizing L2 TLB entries for learning instead of sampling.
target_text: This paper focuses on speeding up virtual-memory address translation, where TLB misses can trigger expensive page walks. The authors combine an Agile TLB Prefetcher with a prediction-based replacement policy to better anticipate which page-table entries will be needed and which cached entries are most useful to keep. The overall goal is to reduce translation overhead and improve memory-system performance beyond traditional replacement strategies such as LRU.

target_text: This paper measures 5G uplink performance in a large football stadium and shows that uploading data can remain a major bottleneck even in modern dense deployments. Using field measurements collected before and during a game, the authors find that high-frequency TDD bands struggle in the uplink because phones face strong propagation loss and receive relatively few uplink time slots. As a result, these bands may perform well for downloads but still provide weak upload capacity. The study concludes that lower-frequency FDD bands end up carrying much of the uplink burden. In plain terms, the paper shows why crowded venues can still have poor upload performance even when headline 5G downlink speeds look impressive.

The core idea is to replace a fixed quadratic loss with an adaptive robust loss function, while adding regularization so the estimator does not fall into trivial solutions. target_text: This paper studies machine-learning attacks on the Learning With Errors problem, a core hardness assumption in lattice-based cryptography. Prior ML attacks worked best on relatively sparse secrets. The authors show that using larger datasets and repeated examples can push those attacks toward denser secrets, and they describe an empirical power-law relationship between recovery success, dataset size, and repetition. They also introduce a stepwise regression method meant to recover additional parts of the secret. The work does not break modern cryptography outright, but it does probe where some LWE variants may become more vulnerable, which is useful for stress-testing post-quantum systems and understanding the limits of ML-based cryptanalysis.

target_text: This paper introduces FAVE, a recommendation method designed to generate the next item in a user sequence more efficiently than diffusion-style approaches. The authors argue that many flow-based recommenders still waste computation because they start from uninformative noise and spend effort modeling transitions that are partly deterministic. FAVE instead learns a direct one-step trajectory from a more informative starting point, using a two-stage training process that first builds a stable semantic space and then adds a semantic anchor prior plus a learned average velocity. The goal is to keep strong recommendation quality while reducing generation cost. In simple terms, the paper tries to make sequence recommendation both faster and more purposeful by starting closer to where the answer should be.

[TITLE] Subset Balancing and Generalized Subset Sum via Lattices
[ABSTRACT] We study the \emph{Subset Balancing} problem: given $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ and a coefficient set $C \subseteq \mathbb{Z}$, find a nonzero vector $\mathbf{c} \in C^n$ such that $\mathbf{c}\cdot\mathbf{x} = 0$. The standard meet-in-the-middle algorithm runs in time $\tilde{O}(|C|^{n/2})=\tilde{O}(2^{n\log |C|/2})$, and recent improvements (SODA~2022, Chen, Jin, Randolph, and Servedio; STOC~2026, Randolph and Węgrzycki) beyond this barrier apply mainly when $d$ is constant. target_text: This paper studies the Subset Balancing problem and gives new algorithms based on lattice reductions. The key result is a reduction from subset balancing with coefficients in a bounded integer range to a single instance of an infinity-norm shortest vector problem. Using the best known lattice algorithms for that target problem, the authors obtain running times that depend on the dimension but no longer pay the usual log d cost in the exponent. For sufficiently large coefficient ranges, they also show the problem becomes polynomial-time solvable. The paper is mainly a complexity-theory advance, but the practical message is that a careful geometric reformulation can lead to substantially better algorithms than the classic meet-in-the-middle approach for many parameter settings.
